title: What Goes Into an Asset's Cost
event: Hollis Co. buys a machine: invoice price $80,000, freight-in $2,000, installation $3,000, and test runs $1,500. During unloading, a worker damaged a panel costing $1,200 to repair. Compute the machine's capitalized cost.

computationPaths.narration: Capitalize every cost NECESSARY to get the asset to its location and condition for use. Accidents aren't necessary — they're expenses.

memorize.body: Asset cost = purchase price + all costs necessary to bring it to intended location and condition (freight, installation, testing, nonrefundable taxes).
The necessity test kills the tempting items: repair of damage, training costs, and insurance AFTER the in-service date are all expenses.

questions.prompt: Invoice $80,000, freight $2,000, installation $3,000, testing $1,500, accident repair $1,200. Capitalized cost = ?

distractors.feedback: You capitalized the accident repair. Damage isn't NECESSARY to ready the asset — it's an expense: 86,500.
Invoice price alone. Freight, installation, and testing are all part of getting the machine ready: 86,500.
